title: Configuration
description: Configure you-agent-factory with factory.json topology: work types, workers, workstations, resources, and how the pieces fit.
openingSummary: factory.json is the topology root for a you-agent-factory run. It declares the work types, workers, workstations, optional resources, and routing the runtime turns into executable workflow.

whatItCovers.title: What It Covers
whatItCovers.body: Configuration is the web overview of factory.json topology for you-agent-factory. It explains what the factory root file owns, how the recommended directory layout splits workers, workstations, and inputs, and how work moves through workstations and workers. Use it when you need the factory topology picture before opening the deeper workers, workstations, or resources references.

keyConcepts.title: Key Concepts
keyConcepts.body: factory.json declares the workflow topology for a you-agent-factory run. It names the work types and their lifecycle states, the workers that do the work, the workstations that dispatch and route outcomes, and optional resources that bound concurrency. The runtime turns that declaration into executable workflow. Split files under workers/ and workstations/ hold the runtime instructions those entries call. Watched submission data lives under inputs/. Operator model defaults in ~/.you-agent-factory/config.json are separate from this factory topology.

whatLivesWhere.title: What Lives Where
whatLivesWhere.body: Put topology in factory.json. Keep worker runtime instructions in workers/<name>/AGENTS.md and workstation runtime instructions in workstations/<name>/AGENTS.md. Drop watched single-work-type requests under inputs/<work-type>/default/, and mixed or relation-heavy batch files under inputs/BATCH/default/. The recommended factory directory layout looks like this:

howPiecesFit.title: How The Pieces Fit
howPiecesFit.body: Work enters the factory as a token in a work type's initial state. A workstation is enabled when its configured input places have matching tokens. The workstation dispatches to its worker, then routes the token based on the worker outcome:

topLevelFields.title: Top-Level Fields
topLevelFields.body: factory.json owns the customer-facing topology fields below. Required fields define executable workflow. Optional resources bound concurrency. id and runner set factory-level defaults. layout and supportingFiles are non-executable portability metadata — they do not change runtime topology.

minimalFactory.title: Minimal Factory
minimalFactory.body: A minimal factory needs one work type, one worker, and one workstation that moves submitted work from an initial state to a terminal state. Copy this shape, then add matching workers/processor/AGENTS.md and workstations/process/AGENTS.md files:

howToUse.title: How To Use
howToUse.body: Start from factory.json as the topology root, then add split worker and workstation instruction files named to match the entries in that file. Use this page for the layout, fields overview, and minimal factory shape. Open the workers, workstations, and resources documentation when you need field-level contracts for those pieces.

limitsAndAssumptions.title: Limits And Assumptions
limitsAndAssumptions.body: This page is a web topology overview for factory.json. It is not a sync of packaged CLI docs, not the operator model-defaults deep dive, and not the submitting-work, batch, relationships, or harness-support references. Deep worker, workstation, and resource field contracts belong on their sibling documentation pages.

links.fieldWorkTypesDescription: Work categories and lifecycle states. Workstation inputs and outputs must reference these names.
links.fieldWorkersDescription: Worker identities that workstations reference by name. Deep worker-type fields live on the workers documentation page.
links.fieldWorkstationsDescription: Dispatch steps that consume input states and produce output states. Deep workstation-kind fields live on the workstations documentation page.
links.fieldResourcesDescription: Optional bounded concurrency pools. Workers and workstations declare requirements against these pools. Deep pool contracts live on the resources documentation page.
links.fieldIdDescription: Factory-level identifier. Prompt context uses this when a submitted work item does not carry a project tag.
links.fieldRunnerDescription: Factory-level default runner ID. Supported built-ins include codex, gemini, kiro, cursor-cli, and opencode.
links.fieldLayoutDescription: Non-executable portable graph-editor layout metadata. It must not change runtime topology or execution behavior.
links.fieldSupportingFilesDescription: Portability-only manifest for validation-only external tools and bundled files. Distinct from runtime-capacity resources.

layoutNotes.body: Inline runtime fields in factory.json still work for portable single-file configs, but the split layout is the recommended authoring path. Live saves persist the split layout: a thin factory.json plus workers/, workstations/, and default inputs/ channels when definitions exist.

placeNote.body: Each workType and state pair becomes a place named <workType>:<state>, such as task:init. That place naming is how workstation inputs and outputs connect to the work-type lifecycle.

fieldsOverviewNote.body: Runtime topology is workTypes, workers, workstations, and optional resources. layout and supportingFiles travel with the factory for portability and presentation only. Do not treat them as executable workflow fields.

minimalFactoryNote.body: Accepted work moves through outputs to task:complete. Failures route through onFailure to task:failed. Add onContinue or onRejection only when those outcomes are part of the workstation contract.
